does: This paper demonstrates that machine unlearning in language models fails catastrophically under quantization, a critical gap since deployed models are always quantized. The authors identify the root cause: standard unlearning methods produce per-parameter updates 47-828x smaller than quantization bin widths, meaning changes cannot survive compression to lower precision. They formalize this as a sparsity-permanence tradeoff where methods achieving meaningful forgetting either lose it under quantization or barely modify the model at all. To resolve both failure modes simultaneously, they propose MANSU, which combines causal circuit attribution to identify minimal forget-relevant subgraphs, applies circuit-restricted null-space projection with diagonal-Fisher retention bounds, and enforces per-parameter magnitude floors to guarantee quantization-persistent unlearning.
connects: This work builds on the emerging intersection of mechanistic interpretability and machine unlearning. It extends prior findings showing that post-training quantization can reverse unlearning by diagnosing why this occurs systematically rather than treating it as a tuning artifact. The paper connects to circuit attribution methods from mechanistic interpretability that isolate causal subnetworks, null-space projection approaches from prior unlearning work that preserve task performance, and recent awareness that quantization introduces additional constraints on learned behavior. It responds to the gap between how unlearning is evaluated in academia, where models are full-precision and freshly trained, versus deployment reality where quantization is ubiquitous and degrades unlearning effectiveness.
enables: This work establishes quantization-permanence as a new evaluation criterion for unlearning methods, pushing the field toward practical deployability standards. It opens directions for designing unlearning algorithms with quantization-awareness built in from the start rather than as post-hoc fixes. The sparsity-permanence tradeoff formalizes a fundamental tension that likely applies beyond unlearning to other targeted model modifications. Future work could explore whether circuit-attribution-based approaches scale to larger models and more complex forget sets, whether similar mechanisms explain other modifications that fail under quantization, and how to design quantization schemes that better preserve interpretable circuit structure. The mechanistic foundation may also inform why certain model behaviors are particularly brittle to compression.
